danger_signals.meaning: Immediate combat threat; player must engage or evade

danger_signals.visual_form: HUD health bar reduction (NES action game standard)
danger_signals.meaning: Player character taking damage; risk of death state

safety_signals.visual_form: Empty screen after defeating all enemies in zone

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Distinct sprite designs for different enemy types (thugs, bosses)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Urban crime-scene environments (streets, buildings, crime locations)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemies spawned on screen; dynamic sprite positioning
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies patrol, attack, and pursue player; combat mechanics activated

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Screen scrolling or level transitions between crime scenes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player advances through sequential environments; narrative progression through action

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Larger, distinct sprite design; centered screen positioning
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss-specific attack patterns; heightened difficulty and extended combat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Text-based dialogue/narrative (NES-era presentation at level transitions)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game pauses for story context; player motivation reinforced through narrative framing

environmental_storytelling.example: Each stage set in recognizable criminal underworld locations (urban streets, crime family establishments) that communicate the Punisher's war against organized crime
Different sprite designs for thugs, henchmen, and bosses signal organizational hierarchy and escalating threat levels within the criminal network
Increasing enemy density and complexity across stages visually conveys expanding criminal operations that the Punisher must dismantle

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Predictable movement patterns; stationary or looping paths
Sprite animation acceleration; movement toward player; attack sequences
Complex multi-phase attack patterns; sprite transformations or animation states indicating specific move telegraphs
Knockback animation; temporary invulnerability flash (standard NES action game visual convention)

notes: The Punisher (NES, 1991) communicates world-state primarily through traditional NES action-game visual language: sprite-based enemy presence signals danger, cleared zones signal safety, and environmental variety (crime scenes) establishes the Punisher's crusade narrative. The game relies on implicit faction communication—the player IS the Punisher (singular protagonist), opposing generic criminal forces with no distinctive faction markers beyond visual enemy variety. Political situation is communicated narratively rather than visually: organized crime vs. vigilante justice. The 'creepy face' Easter Egg (hidden expression animation) represents developer personality/easter egg culture rather than mechanical communication. Limited source material restricts detailed manifestation analysis.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - The Punisher NES article)
